s: An 18th-century tower said to have haunted Tolkien's imagination
w: John Perrott built this 96-foot tower in 1758 for reasons no one agreed on — to survey land, spot game, or see his wife's grave 15 miles away. From 1884 it served as a meteorological station. Tolkien grew up minutes away; scholars link it and the nearby Edgbaston Waterworks chimney to the dark towers of Mordor. Claim unconfirmed. Grade II* listed.
_facts: Built in 1758 by John Perrott in what was then open Rotton Park
Stands 29 metres (96 feet) tall; Grade II* listed
Served as a Birmingham and Midland Institute meteorological station from 1884 to 1979
Scholars suggest it and the Edgbaston Waterworks may have influenced Tolkien's towers; Tolkien lived nearby as a child
Also known as 'The Monument' and 'The Observatory'

s: The 'lost paradise' that gave Tolkien his ancient forests
w: Tolkien spent ages four to eight near this peat bog and later called it 'a kind of lost paradise — a dell, a sandpit, a pool with swans, and a mill that really did grind corn.' In 1966 he told the Guardian: 'I always knew it would go — and it did.' It didn't. Campaigners saved the woodland in 1980 under its new name; before that it was just 'The Dell.'
_facts: Tolkien lived within walking distance as a child around 1896-1900
He described it as 'a kind of lost paradise' in a 1966 Guardian interview
Credited as inspiration for ancient forests in The Lord of the Rings and The Hobbit
Formerly called 'The Dell'; renamed Moseley Bog after the 1980 'Save Our Bog' campaign
Part of the Shire Country Park alongside Sarehole Mill; 12 hectares, Local Nature Reserve

s: The mill Tolkien turned into Hobbiton — still turning on the River Cole
w: Tolkien lived 300 yards from this 1771 water mill between the ages of four and eight, and said it was the mill he had in mind when he wrote about Hobbiton. Matthew Boulton leased the site in the 1750s for metal-rolling and wire-drawing. One of only two working water mills left in Birmingham. Now a museum; Grade II listed.
_facts: Current mill building dates from 1771; the site has had a mill since 1542
Tolkien lived within 300 yards as a child and confirmed it as inspiration for the Mill at Hobbiton
Matthew Boulton leased it c.1755 for metal-rolling, wire-drawing and tool-sharpening
One of only two working water mills remaining in Birmingham
Operated as a museum by Birmingham Museums Trust; Grade II listed

s: The factory that furnished the coffins of Churchill, Diana, and two kings
w: From 1894 this Fleet Street factory made the brass handles and silk linings for the most scrutinised funerals in Britain — Churchill, George V, George VI, the Queen Mother, Princess Diana. When cremation made metal fittings illegal, the firm stopped in 1998 and locked the door. The 2014 museum found overalls on hooks, tools on benches, ledgers open. Grade II* listed.
_facts: Founded 1882 as a brass foundry; moved to Fleet Street 1894 and switched to coffin furniture
Made fittings for funerals of Churchill, Chamberlain, George V, George VI, Queen Mother, and Princess Diana
Employed around 100 workers at peak in the 1950s-60s; exported to West Africa, India, Sri Lanka, the Caribbean
Closed 1998 when cremation legislation banned metal fittings in cremations
Museum opened October 2014 after 15-year campaign by Birmingham Conservation Trust; Grade II* listed

s: Two rival canals met here — separated by a 7-foot plank for 40 years
w: When the Worcester and Birmingham Canal arrived here in the 1790s, the Birmingham Canal Navigations Company refused to connect the two. They built the Worcester Bar — a 7-foot-wide barrier 84 yards long — so neither company lost toll revenue. Goods had to be unloaded by hand. Parliament ended the stand-off in 1815. The basin now sits at the heart of Birmingham's 100-mile canal network.
_facts: The Worcester Bar was a physical barrier 84 yards long and 7 feet 3 inches wide dividing the two canal companies
Goods had to be manually transferred between boats on either side until 1815, when Parliament mandated a connecting lock
Birmingham Canal Navigations operated from 1767; the wider BCN network covers just over 100 miles today
Gas Street Basin featured in the 1973 Cliff Richard film Take Me High and in the soap opera Crossroads
Several structures around the basin are Grade II listed

s: The last surviving court of back-to-backs, frozen across four eras
w: Court 15 on Inge Street housed over 500 families between 1802 and the 1970s — button-makers, pearl button drillers, a Windrush-generation tailor. Four houses are restored to four decades: 1840s, 1870s, 1930s, 1970s. After the Public Health Act 1875 outlawed new back-to-backs, Birmingham demolished the rest. These are all that survive. National Trust; guided tours only.
_facts: Court 14 completed 1802 by Joseph Willmore; Hurst Street terrace added 1831
Over 500 families occupied the court through the 19th century in trades including button-making, glasswork, leatherwork and jewellery
Four houses restored to four distinct periods: 1840s, 1870s, 1930s, and 1970s
The last surviving court of back-to-back houses in Birmingham after the Public Health Act 1875 banned their construction
Managed by the National Trust since 2004; accessible by guided tour only

s: Britain's oldest working cinema — opened 1909, closed 2024
w: The Electric opened on Station Street on 27 December 1909, showing silent pictures to Edwardian Birmingham. It outlasted talkies, the Blitz, television, and the multiplex age — 114 years in all — to become the oldest continuously operating cinema in Britain. One screen kept its 35mm projector. It closed on 29 February 2024. The Grade II listed building remains.
_facts: Opened 27 December 1909 on Station Street, Birmingham — Birmingham's first cinema
Held the title of oldest working cinema in Britain until its closure on 29 February 2024
Operated two screens, one retaining 35mm projection capability alongside digital
Grade II listed building; Arts and Crafts terrace facade

s: Two-tier catacombs and the wandering remains of a great atheist printer
w: Opened in 1847, this cemetery is built around two tiers of catacombs whose 'unhealthy vapours' triggered the Birmingham Cemeteries Act — all non-interred coffins must be sealed with lead or pitch. John Baskerville, typeface designer and avowed atheist, ended up here in 1897, disinterred twice since burial on his own land. His family petitioned in 1963 to move him to unconsecrated ground. They failed.
_facts: Opened 1847; closed to new burials 1982; one of two cemeteries in the Jewellery Quarter
Features two tiers of catacombs; 'unhealthy vapours' prompted Birmingham Cemeteries Act requiring sealed coffins
John Baskerville (typeface designer, 1706-1775) reinterred here in 1897 after earlier burial site disrupted by canal construction
Harry Gem (1819-1881), pioneer of lawn tennis, is also buried here
Also known as Brookfields Cemetery, Church of England Cemetery, and Mint Cemetery; contains 64 Commonwealth war graves

s: Built for 20,000 pounds, it came in at 5,012 — then got Burne-Jones windows
w: Thomas Archer built this Baroque church between 1711 and 1715, studying Roman churches beforehand. The estimate was 20,000 pounds; materials came in donated, bringing the bill to 5,012. Edward Burne-Jones, baptised here, gave the four great stained-glass windows. They were removed at the start of the Second World War, survived, and were reinstalled undamaged in 1948. It became a cathedral in 1905.
_facts: Built 1711-1715 by Thomas Archer in Baroque style; consecrated 1715; Grade I listed
Cost only 5,012 pounds instead of the estimated 20,000 because materials were donated and transported free
Edward Burne-Jones was baptised here and donated four stained-glass windows including three at the eastern end
Windows were removed for safekeeping in WWII, survived undamaged, and reinstalled in 1948 after bomb damage was repaired
Became the cathedral of the Diocese of Birmingham in 1905

s: A gold workshop locked up in 1981 — tools still on the benches
w: The Smith and Pepper family ran this Vyse Street gold jewellery workshop for over 80 years, changing almost nothing. When the elderly owners retired in 1981 they locked the door, leaving tools on the benches, overalls on hooks, and jars of Marmite in the cupboard. The workshop opened as a museum in 1992. The Jewellery Quarter around it still makes 40 per cent of all UK jewellery.
_facts: Smith and Pepper family-run gold jewellery workshop operated for over 80 years with minimal changes to tools or methods
Owners simply 'locked the door' on retirement in 1981, leaving the workshop completely intact
Opened as Jewellery Quarter Discovery Centre in 1992; refurbished and reopened 2025 after 2020 closure
The wider Jewellery Quarter produces 40% of all UK jewellery and is Europe's largest concentration of jewellery trade businesses
Located at 70-80 Vyse Street; managed by Birmingham Museums Trust

s: The 'Westminster Abbey of the Midlands' — nondenominational since 1836
w: When Key Hill opened in 1836 it was the first cemetery in Birmingham outside a churchyard and the first to accept all denominations. By 1915 it was 'the Westminster Abbey of the Midlands': Joseph Chamberlain, Harriet Martineau, George Dawson the reform preacher, and Alfred Bird — who invented custard because his wife was allergic to eggs — are all buried here. The Greek Doric chapel has gone; Charles Edge's gate piers survive.
_facts: Opened 23 May 1836 as Birmingham General Cemetery; oldest non-churchyard cemetery in the city
Described as 'the Westminster Abbey of the Midlands' in 1915
Notable burials: Joseph Chamberlain (politician/mayor), Harriet Martineau (author), Alfred Bird (chemist/custard inventor), George Dawson (reformer)
Designed by architect Charles Edge; entrance gates and railings are Grade II listed
Cemetery holds Grade II* status on the Register of Historic Parks and Gardens; contains 46 Commonwealth war graves

s: Alfred Bird invented custard here because his wife was allergic to eggs
w: Alfred Bird opened his pharmacy on this site in 1837 and solved a domestic problem: his wife Elizabeth was allergic to eggs. He made her custard using cornflour instead. Guests tasted it by accident and loved it. The factory his son built covered 15 acres and employed 1,000 people before moving to Banbury in 1964. The Victorian brick complex reopened in 1992 as creative workspace; ASOS and Codemasters both started here.
_facts: Alfred Bird (1811-1878) invented egg-free custard in 1837 because his wife Elizabeth was allergic to eggs
Also invented baking powder in 1843, again to accommodate his wife's yeast allergy
The Bird's Custard Factory covered 15 acres at its peak and employed around 1,000 workers
Factory relocated to Banbury in 1964 when the Birmingham site became uneconomical
Redeveloped from 1992 as creative workspace; early tenants included ASOS, Codemasters, and Rare

s: Where Watt, Darwin, Priestley and Wedgwood dined by the full moon
w: Matthew Boulton moved into Soho House in 1766 and began hosting the Lunar Society — a dinner club that met near the full moon so members could see to ride home. Around his table: James Watt, Erasmus Darwin, Joseph Priestley, Josiah Wedgwood. They called themselves the Lunaticks. Between Sunday dinners they laid the groundwork for the Industrial Revolution. Boulton died here in 1809.
_facts: Matthew Boulton leased and moved into Soho House in 1766, living there until his death in 1809
The Lunar Society met here monthly near the full moon; members included Watt, Erasmus Darwin, Priestley, and Wedgwood
Members called themselves 'Lunaticks' as a play on the contemporary spelling of lunatics
Meetings ran from 2pm on Sundays with dinner and discussions continuing until at least 8pm
Now operated as a museum by Birmingham Museums Trust

s: Home to the world's largest collection of Edward Burne-Jones
w: Opened in 1885 with the motto 'By the gains of Industry we promote Art', this gallery grew to around 800,000 objects. Its singular prize is the world's largest holding of Edward Burne-Jones, the Pre-Raphaelite born in the city. The Round Room holds Jacob Epstein's brooding Archangel Lucifer. Bombed in 1940, rewired 2020-2024; back open.
_facts: Officially opened 28 November 1885 by the Prince of Wales; first public exhibition space established 1864
Holds the world's largest collection of works by Edward Burne-Jones, a Birmingham native
Collection spans approximately 800,000 objects across fine art, ceramics, natural history, archaeology and industrial heritage
The Round Room features Jacob Epstein's sculpture The Archangel Lucifer
Seven galleries required rebuilding after 1940 bombing; closed 2020-2024 for structural rewiring, reopened October 2024

s: A Victorian board school turned one of England's best contemporary art spaces
w: John Henry Chamberlain designed this neo-Gothic board school in 1877, complete with a tower later demolished. Ikon — founded in 1965 by a Birmingham School of Art collective — moved in after a 1997 refurbishment that restored the missing tower. By the late 1970s it was already one of the most important contemporary art galleries outside London. Olafur Eliasson, Cornelia Parker, Diane Arbus have all shown here.
_facts: Building is the Oozells Street Board School, designed by John Henry Chamberlain in 1877; Grade II listed neo-Gothic
Ikon Gallery founded in 1965 by Angus Skene and four Birmingham School of Art artists
Moved to Oozells Street in 1997 after refurbishment by Levitt Bernstein, which restored the demolished tower
By the late 1970s rated 'one of the most important contemporary art galleries outside London'
Has shown Olafur Eliasson, Cornelia Parker, Richard Billingham, Julian Opie, and early travelling shows by Diane Arbus

n: St Martin in the Bull Ring
s: Birmingham's mother church — where bells were rung to drown John Wesley
w: Documented in 1263, St Martin's is the church around which Birmingham grew. In 1745, churchwardens ordered the bells rung to drown out John Wesley preaching in the street outside. Medieval wall paintings were found during Victorian demolition in 1873. The 1643 Parliamentary attack left a cannonball hole in the staircase banister that no one has ever repaired. Grade II* listed.
_facts: Documented in 1263; Birmingham's original parish church; Grade II* listed
Spire struck by lightning three times by 1808
In 1745, churchwardens had the bells rung to drown out John Wesley preaching outdoors
Medieval wall paintings discovered during 1873 demolition, including imagery of St Martin dividing his cloak
A 1643 Parliamentary cannonball left a hole in the staircase banister still visible today

s: A Loudon design from 1829 with a fern found nowhere else on Earth
w: John Claudius Loudon laid out these 15 acres in 1829 and the gardens opened in 1832; most of his original design survives. The glasshouses hold over 7,000 plant varieties, among them the fern Dicksonia x lathamii — created by curator W. B. Latham over 140 years ago and found nowhere else in the world. The British National Bonsai Collection includes a Japanese specimen over 250 years old. Grade II* listed.
_facts: Designed by John Claudius Loudon in 1829 for the Birmingham Botanical and Horticultural Society; opened 11 June 1832
15-acre site on the Calthorpe estate in Edgbaston; Grade II* listed on the Register of Historic Parks and Gardens
The fern Dicksonia x lathamii, created by curator W.B. Latham, exists nowhere else in the world
Home to the British National Bonsai Collection (opened 1993), including a 250-year-old Japanese specimen
Ernest Henry Wilson, later a renowned plant collector, trained here in the 1890s

s: The factory that made the police whistle, the ref's whistle, and the Titanic's
w: Joseph Hudson won the Metropolitan Police whistle contract in 1883 by inventing a small pea whistle to replace hand rattles. He had already made the first football referee whistle, replacing handkerchiefs. His Acme Thunderer became the world's best-selling whistle. Whistles from this Barr Street factory went down with the Titanic in 1912. The company still operates here, making five million a year — over a billion since it started.
_facts: Joseph Hudson won the Metropolitan Police whistle contract in 1883, replacing hand rattles with the pea whistle
Hudson also invented the first football referee whistle, replacing handkerchiefs
His Acme Thunderer became the world's best-selling whistle model
Whistles manufactured here went down with the RMS Titanic in 1912; Acme made replica whistles for the 2012 centenary
Factory at 244 Barr Street, Hockley; still produces approximately 5 million whistles per year and over 1 billion total

s: A Jacobean mansion with a Civil War cannonball still in the banister
w: Sir Thomas Holte built this prodigy house 1618-1635, just in time for Parliamentary forces to attack it in 1643. A cannonball punched a hole in the staircase banister that has never been repaired. Washington Irving visited and based Bracebridge Hall on it; James Watt's son later leased it. In 1864, Birmingham Corporation bought it — the first historic country house in England to pass into municipal ownership.
_facts: Built 1618-1635 by Sir Thomas Holte; Grade I listed Jacobean prodigy house designed by John Thorpe
Attacked by Parliamentary forces in 1643; cannonball hole in the staircase banister remains unrepaired
Washington Irving visited and used it as inspiration for Bracebridge Hall
James Watt Jr. (son of the steam engine engineer) leased the hall after 1817
Purchased by Birmingham Corporation in 1864, the first historic country house to pass into municipal ownership in England; rated UK's top haunted heritage site in 2019
